file_name: IntegrityFunds_20200121_485BPOS_EX-99.E UNDR CONTR_11948727_EX-99.E UNDR CONTR_Service Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is terminable with respect to the Fund, without penalty, (a) on 60 days' written notice, by vote of a majority of the Qualified Trustees or by vote of a majority (as defined in the 1940 Act) of the outstanding voting securities of the Fund or (b) on 90 days' written notice by Integrity.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is terminable with respect to the Fund, without penalty, (a) on 15 days' written notice, by vote of a majority of the Qualified Trustees or by vote of a majority (as defined in the 1940 Act) of the outstanding voting securities of the Fund or (b) on 30 days' written notice by Integrity.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 15 days for the Fund and 30 days for Integrity creates a legal contradiction, as it may not provide sufficient time for the fund to find a replacement underwriter, especially given the complexities involved in transferring regulatory responsibilities.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(3) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that contracts with investment advisers and principal underwriters be approved annually by the fund's board or shareholders and that these contracts can be terminated with 'no more than sixty days' written notice'. Reducing the notice period to 15 days for the Fund and 30 days for Integrity violates this requirement.

Contracts of advisers and underwriters (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the termination notice period for both the Fund (from 60 to 15 days) and Integrity (from 90 to 30 days). This contradicts Section 15(a)(3) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, which, as stated in the Law Explanation, requires that contracts with investment advisers and principal underwriters be terminable with 'no more than sixty days' written notice. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 (a)(3), confirms this, stating that the contract 'provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time... on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser'. The reduction to 15 and 30 days violates this explicit requirement, making the change a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Fund's agreement to indemnify Integrity, its officers and governors, and any such controlling person, as aforesaid, is expressly conditioned upon the Fund's being notified of any action brought against Integrity, its officers or governors, or any such controlling person, such notification to be given by letter or by telegram addressed to the Fund at its principal office in Minot, North Dakota, and sent to the Fund by the person against whom such action is brought, within ten days after the summons or other first legal process shall have been served.
perturbation.changed_text: The Fund's agreement to indemnify Integrity, its officers and governors, and any such controlling person, as aforesaid, is expressly conditioned upon the Fund's being notified of any action brought against Integrity, its officers or governors, or any such controlling person, such notification to be given by letter or by telegram addressed to the Fund at its principal office in Minot, North Dakota, and sent to the Fund by the person against whom such action is brought, within two days after the summons or other first legal process shall have been served.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for indemnification claims to two days is an inconsistency. This timeframe is so short that it places an unreasonable burden on Integrity (or related parties) to notify the Fund, potentially jeopardizing their indemnification rights if they miss the extremely tight deadline.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 17 CFR § 270.17d-1 doesn't explicitly state a notification period, it implies that joint transactions, including indemnification agreements, must be fair and not disadvantageous to the investment company. A two-day notification period for legal action would be considered an unreasonable burden. Therefore, it would make the contract disadvantagous.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 270.17d-1 - Applications regarding joint enterprises or arrangements and certain profit-sharing plans. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 270.17d-1 - Applications regarding joint enterprises or arrangements and certain profit-sharing plans. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 270.17d-1 Applications regarding joint enterprises or arrangements and certain profit-sharing plans. (a) No affiliated person of or principal underwriter for any registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 12(d)(3) (A) and (B) of the Act) and no affiliated person of such a person or principal underwriter , acting as principal, shall participate in, or effect any transaction in connection with, any joint enterprise or other joint arrangement or profit-sharing plan in which any such registered company, or a company controlled by such registered company, is a participant, and which is entered into, adopted or modified subsequent to the effective date of this rule , unless an application regarding such joint enterprise, arrangement or profit-sharing plan has been filed with the Commission and has been granted by an order entered prior to the submission of such plan or modification to security holders for approval, or prior to such adoption or modification if not so submitted, except that the provisions of this rule shall not preclude any affiliated person from acting as manager of any underwriting syndicate or other group in which such registered or controlled company is a participant and receiving compensation therefor. (b) In passing upon such applications, the Commission will consider whether the participation of such registered or controlled company in such joint enterprise, joint arrangement or profit-sharing plan on the basis proposed is consistent with the provisions, policies and purposes of the Act and the extent to wh
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification period to two days, which creates an unreasonable burden according to the perturbation explanation and law explanation. 17 CFR § 270.17d-1, as referenced in the Law Explanation, implies that joint transactions must be fair. The two-day notification period is considered an unreasonable burden on Integrity. The change contradicts the standard of fairness required in joint transactions, making the contract potentially disadvantageous.

perturbation.original_text: Section 7.1 This Agreement shall continue until January 18, 2022, and thereafter shall continue automatically for successive annual periods ending on January 18th of each year, provided such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by (a) the Fund's Board of Trustees and (b) a vote of a majority (as defined in the 1940 Act) of the Fund's Trustees who are not interested persons (as defined in the 1940 Act) of the Fund and who have no direct or indirect financial interest in the operation of the Plan, in this Agreement, or any agreement related to the Plan (the "Qualified Trustees"), by vote cast in person at a meeting called for the purpose of voting on such approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 7.1 This Agreement shall continue until January 18, 2022, and thereafter shall continue automatically for successive annual periods ending on January 18th of each year, provided such continuance is specifically approved every five years by (a) the Fund's Board of Trustees and (b) a vote of a majority (as defined in the 1940 Act) of the Fund's Trustees who are not interested persons (as defined in the 1940 Act) of the Fund and who have no direct or indirect financial interest in the operation of the Plan, in this Agreement, or any agreement related to the Plan (the "Qualified Trustees"), by vote cast in person at a meeting called for the purpose of voting on such approval.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the required approval period for the agreement's continuation from 'at least annually' to 'every five years' directly contradicts the requirements for annual review and approval of underwriting agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 explicitly requires that the board of directors of a registered investment company, including a majority of the independent directors, must evaluate and approve the terms of the underwriting agreement 'at least annually'. Modifying the contract to allow for review every five years violates this statutory mandate.

(b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the agreement's continuation approval period from 'at least annually' to 'every five years'. This directly contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 (a)(2), which states that the contract 'shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors'. The modification weakens a core legal obligation by changing a mandatory annual review to a review every five years.
